title: Assignment One
text:
Visual information is perhaps the most potent form of human understanding. This is because we are ‘designed’ to interact with our world primarily via our sense of sight, like many other primates. Hence our species’ great works of art appeal to this dimension of subjective experience more than they do that of, say, smell.


For more information on our visual heritage, see: Kaas JH, Balaram P. Current research on the organization and function of the visual system in primates. Eye Brain. 2014;6(Suppl):1-4. doi: 10.2147/EB.S64016. PMID: 26388695; PMCID: PMC4574956.
For an instance of the ability of the eyes to capture the mind, check out this AI ‘generated’ book-cover artwork for an essay set published by the LessWrong community:



An example of striking generative artwork. You can click the image to visit the original blog post. Pace, Ben. 2021. “Book Launch: The Engines of Cognition.” https://www.lesswrong.com/posts/ mvPfao35Moah8py46/book-launch-the-engines-of-cognition (Retrieved on September 10, 2024).


Each of these covers was produced with the help of of ML module that uses text prompts to alter a piece of base art.
Visuals can also help us to express other types of information as well. Such as relationships, comparisons, as well as the type of things usually dealt with in more information based enterprises, like statistics. For example, let us examine a piece of graphical output from a publication on the biological underpinning of human memory:



Correlate graph, see main text for details. Kandel, Eric R., Yadin Dudai, and Mark R. Mayford. 2014. “The Molecular and Systems Biology of Memory.” Cell 157(1): 163–86. doi:10.1016/j.cell.2014.03.001.


There is certianly a lot going on here. But there is definitely some structure as well. The left-most panel appears to concern itself with modeling an exemplar of the human brain, either an actual or an idealized one. The coloration and the subtitle seem to indicate the wish to convey some sort of effect localized to particular regions of the brain. The right-most panel appears to display some sort of nodal relationship between whatever SFG, PCN, IPL and all the other three-letter labels are. Primarily it seems to wish to get across ‘% connectivity’, as evidenced by the blue-red color spectrum key on the far right end.





An example of a code generated image from an R script published by Fronkonstin.com


A glance at the other sub-labels indicates that these relationships appears to have something to do with ‘spatial’ and ‘temporal’ relationships within a specified range. So, a quick guess is that this output wants to convey some association between some ‘nodes’ of some sort that are so linked via space and/or time, and that are either the cause of, the outcome of, or at least co-terminal with some changes in a human brain.
Indeed, something like is indeed the case. The graphic in question displays connections between different regions of the brain (the SPLs, etc) during the formation and retrieval of memories as measured by ECoG recording. The panels are clearly delineated, and use vivid color in order to help parse apart relationships that are supposed to be noticed as significant.
Of course, it might be argued that this panel is only easily interpretable in situ. In fact, the lack of a ‘key’ identifying brain regions within the panel itself would take this criticism to the point of observation; but perhaps not any further. Because to some extent, that is true of a majority of graphic visualizations of data (or anything else). Context conditions understanding - the best test is how well something stands up within it’s intended domain. All in all, this graphic successfully applies well-ordered design principles in order to convey interrelations in an intuitive manner.
Data visuals can reveal underlying patterns: Replicating a paper by Anscombe (1973) shows how different data sets can lead to the same OLS estimate. The fact that set ‘x4’ and ‘x1’ will give one the same betas should give us pause…

title: Assignment Two
text:
While pursuing the enterprise of living, we are confronted and confront ourselves with ideations of greater of less meaning to the next moment. We most often confront a stacked series of such concerns. We confront trends. While existence is a presentary phenomenon, thinking through trends can only reflect anticipations – about another instance in time or another motion of some thing (even of that or of those that will not happen or will not be).
While correlation does not equate to causation, seeing is believing – even if that belief is only a provisional insight that makes room for a conceptually more rigorous understanding later. Perhaps most ideas serve their purpose via a sort of theoretical senescence…
So, the provision of raw data with which to ‘see’ some aspect of our world can be appreciated as a sort of thought-nursery. There is probably no more bustling nursery of information than whatever resides in people that leads them to search for stuff on Google. We can look at a fresh stock of such crop below:


For more on that sort of thing, see: Colin Ware. 2022. Visual Thinking for Information Design. Elsevier. doi:10.1016/B978-0-12-823567-6.00011-2.





















The above are some charts made in excel from Google trend data on the search terms ‘Trump’, ‘election’ and ‘Harris’ on 9/28/24. The left one represents each terms relative popularity over the last hour, the right one a longer view over the last 30 days.
We can seen in both time-frames that ‘Trump’ tops the results. It seems that ‘Trump’ especially dominates over the longer term. We can also see the impact on visual output that collection procedures have by re-examining the results for ‘Trump’ and ‘Harris’, but by grabbing them directly from Google in R (with different time-stamps to reflect the fact these weren’t taken at the exact same tick o’ the clock):





















We can see similar trends via this procurement method as we saw with the other. The relative difference in popularity between ‘Trump’ and ‘Harris’ hold, even though the search results were sampled hours apart. However, the popularity of each term for a given sub-interval of time differs. It should be expected that contingency differences crop up more often at more granular time-frames (more impactful noise within a smaller sample-size).


Here’s the same approach, but now examining the popularity of the search term ‘immigrants’ across countries over 20 years: 
The word ‘sampling’ is pertinent here. These results constitute a set of data that do not allow one to directly conclude how much interest exists among most Americans in front-runner candidates. The data that was used to produce these charts are best conceived of as convenience samples, and to use them to make inferences about anything other than those using Google during select moments (assuming they aren’t bots) is another enterprise all together. One well-suited to the wider art of data analysis.

title: McKinsey 2023 and D3
text:
Last December, consulting firm McKinsey published a compilation of some of the most telling visualizations they produced over the course of 2023. One graphic caught my eye in particular:





We can create a version of this chart with the code below:

library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)

# Dataset 1: one value per group
data <- data.frame(
  name=c("Career Devolopment","Meaningful Work","Safe Place","Caring Leadership","Geo. Ties","Flexibility","Healthy Support","Taffic Access"),
  val=sample(seq(1,10), 8 )
)

data %>%
  arrange(val) %>%    # First sort by val. This sort the dataframe but NOT the factor levels
  mutate(name=factor(name, levels=name)) %>%   # This trick update the factor levels
  ggplot( aes(x=name, y=val)) +
  geom_segment( aes(xend=name, yend=0)) +
  geom_point( size=4, color="black") +
  coord_flip() +
  theme_minimal() +
  xlab("")







# Reference: https://r-graph-gallery.com/267-reorder-a-variable-in-ggplot2.html

D3 is a JavaScript library that can help professionals develop data visuals. Basically, it is a catalog of charts that can be customized for displaying data sets. They offer a remarkable range of graphics to choose from, but we will just focus on one here: the stacked bar-chart. We can in fact try to recreate a simulacrum of the graphic pictured below:



Our goal



# Using random numbers
library(truncnorm)

nn <- 1e4
set.seed(1)
BimodalDist. <- c(rtruncnorm(nn/2, a=1, b=5, mean=2, sd=.5),
                    rtruncnorm(nn/2, a=1, b=5, mean=4, sd=.5))

# Plot histogram
hist(BimodalDist., freq = FALSE)
# Estimate density
dens <- density(BimodalDist.)
# Overlay density curve
lines(dens, col = "red")







# References: https://www.r-bloggers.com/2023/08/kernel-density-plots-in-r/ & 
# https://stats.stackexchange.com/questions/355344/simulating-a-bimodal-distribution-in-the-range-of-15-in-r

title: Qualtrics Survey Set-Up
text:
Using Qualtrics, an online survey creation and diffusion platform, to implement sound survey design principles is an opportunity to exercise some of the most fundamental tools of social research. Below, I will briefly outline a pedagogically inspired sample survey designed for distribution via Qualtrics.
First, I downloaded the ‘Movie Rental’ .qsf file from GitHub. I then uploaded this file, and imported it into a new survey project of the same name. I also added page-breaks, gave it a new ‘paint’ job, and added some skip logic to select questions that were dependent upon each other. Here is a snippet of the end result:

We can see that the structure of the survey is one that tends to ask questions about how respondents view movies and what types of movies they watch. The survey exhibits a two-tier structure at the macro level - with subject-area questions coming first, and demographic questions coming in at the end. This is standard practice, as putting more routine/less stimulating questions towards the end of a survey rather than the beginning increases the chances that respondents will complete the survey.
Strictly speaking, as noted by Groves et al. (2009), a survey is not more representative of any population but the subset of people who actually respond to it. But we can use the insights of inferential statistics and the assumptions of random sampling to formulate guesses about wider populations that our samples are parts of.
One robust approach to doing this is via an interdisciplinary framework that goes by the name total survey error, or TSE. This methodological perspective combines insights from mathematical statistics and social science to better reduce systematically directional mal-presumptions (biases), random error, and human combobulations (interviewer-sourced errors).
Within TSE, estimates of parameters (standard errors) based upon design structures such as clustering and stratification, relatively sophisticated weighting schemas can be developed to approximate the results of independent and identically distributed (IID) random sampling. Combing this good question design, and coding checks (see Fowler, Jr. (2014)), we may soon be on our way toward knowing who watches what movies, and how.

title: Assignment Three and a Pre-Hackathon
text:
Let us take at look at the break-down of making a histogram in base R. The following code produces one based off of some randomly generated data (from Murrell 2019):

Y <- rnorm(50)
Y[Y < -3.5 | Y > 3.5] <- NA 
x <- seq(-3.5, 3.5, .1)
dn <- dnorm(x)
par(mar=c(4.5, 4.1, 3.1, 0))
hist(Y, breaks=seq(-3.5, 3.5), ylim=c(0, 0.5), 
     col="gray80", freq=FALSE)
lines(x, dnorm(x), lwd=2)







par(mar=c(5.1, 4.1, 4.1, 2.1))

But what exactly does each line of code do? We can break that down via comments to itself, as below:
# Histogram
# Make random data
Y <- rnorm(50)
# Make sure no Y exceed [-3.5, 3.5]
Y[Y < -3.5 | Y > 3.5] <- NA # Selection/set range
x <- seq(-3.5, 3.5, .1) # Set ''x' density range at increments of '.1'
dn <- dnorm(x) # make 'x' a standardized normally distributed variable
par(mar=c(4.5, 4.1, 3.1, 0)) # Set the graphs margins; range of 'y'
hist(Y, breaks=seq(-3.5, 3.5), ylim=c(0, 0.5), # Set 'y' as the variable of interest, 
     col="gray80", freq=FALSE) # Delimit the range, set color, and don't plot as frequency
lines(x, dnorm(x), lwd=2) # draw a line of the 'x' distro conjured four lines above
par(mar=c(5.1, 4.1, 4.1, 2.1)) # set margins for the 'x' line
We can also modify some graphics originally produced in a seminal paper by Anscombe (1973):





















The graphs on the left were produced using base R, while those on the right were made with ggplot2. Emphasizing the versatility of approaches available for graphic modelling in R!

title: Assignment_04
text:
In this article, I will do some PCA (Principal Component) and clustering analysis on U.S. crime data.
With PCA we try to find permutations of a data-set that identifies what relationships are responsible for causing the most uncorrelated variance. Specifically, this transformation is done for ‘dimensions’ of the data by rotating the original data (making it orthogonal and hence uncorrelated to itself) along a new axis in a way that minimizes the average (squared) distance between observations and the new direction so taken. Often, you can work with the first two dimensions that will permit this type of transformation to make comparisons about your data-set.
So, I first load in my data-set:

library(datasets)
library(ISLR)
arrest = USArrests
states = row.names(USArrests)
names(USArrests)

[1] "Murder"   "Assault"  "UrbanPop" "Rape"    


I get the means

apply(USArrests, 2, mean)

  Murder  Assault UrbanPop     Rape 
   7.788  170.760   65.540   21.232 


I also grab the variations

apply(USArrests, 2, var)

    Murder    Assault   UrbanPop       Rape 
  18.97047 6945.16571  209.51878   87.72916 


I then apply PCA with scaling

pr.out = prcomp(USArrests, scale = TRUE)
names(pr.out)

[1] "sdev"     "rotation" "center"   "scale"    "x"       


Then I center the data

pr.out$center

  Murder  Assault UrbanPop     Rape 
   7.788  170.760   65.540   21.232 


I make a ‘variable loadings matrix’

pr.out$scale

   Murder   Assault  UrbanPop      Rape 
 4.355510 83.337661 14.474763  9.366385 


then rotate it

pr.out$rotation

                PC1        PC2        PC3         PC4
Murder   -0.5358995 -0.4181809  0.3412327  0.64922780
Assault  -0.5831836 -0.1879856  0.2681484 -0.74340748
UrbanPop -0.2781909  0.8728062  0.3780158  0.13387773
Rape     -0.5434321  0.1673186 -0.8177779  0.08902432


check dimension size

dim(pr.out$x)

[1] 50  4


Finally, outputting:

pr.out$rotation=-pr.out$rotation
pr.out$x=-pr.out$x
biplot(pr.out, scale=0)








compute the standard deviation

pr.out$sdev

[1] 1.5748783 0.9948694 0.5971291 0.4164494


calculate the variance by squaring and specifiy the proportion of variance explained

pr.var=pr.out$sdev^2
pve=pr.var/sum(pr.var)

plotting:

plot(pve, xlab="Principla Component", ylab = "Proportion of Variance Explained", ylim=c(0,1),type = 'b')








to show the cumulative effect of explanatory power in regard to variance, I use:

plot(cumsum(pve), xlab="Principal Component", ylab="Cumulative Proportion of Variance Explained", ylim=c(0,1),type='b')








Finally, plotting out:

library(factoextra)

Loading required package: ggplot2


Welcome! Want to learn more? See two factoextra-related books at https://goo.gl/ve3WBa

fviz_pca_biplot(pr.out, font.family = "Georgia", col.var = "firebrick1")








Next, I move on to K-Means clustering.

The goal of K-Means is to find the amount of clusters of observations within the data the minimizes the sum of squares within those clusters. Of course, we can also specify the number of clusters as well. First, I load in my new data-set, perform other set-up steps and chart the difference between hard-drive size and ram capacity in each PC:

library(dplyr)
library(ggplot2)
library(RColorBrewer)
library(animation)
computers = read.csv("https://raw.githubusercontent.com/guru99-edu/R-Programming/master/computers.csv")

rescaled_comp <- computers[4:5] %>%
  mutate(hd_scal = scale(hd),
         ram_scal = scale(ram)) %>%
  select(c(hd_scal, ram_scal))
  
ggplot(data = rescaled_comp, aes(x = hd_scal, y = ram_scal)) +
  geom_point(pch=20, col = "blue") + theme_bw() +
  labs(x = "Hard drive size (Scaled)", y ="RAM size (Scaled)" ) +
  theme(text = element_text(family="Georgia")) 








Next, I produce the output of the k-means process via an animation with (producing the output as a sequence of frames):

set.seed(2345)
kmeans.ani(rescaled_comp[1:2], centers = 4, pch = 15:18, col = 1:4)







































































Next, I try the k-means approach with some flower data via the ‘Iris’ data-set:

ggplot(iris, aes(Petal.Length, Petal.Width, color = Species)) + geom_point() + 
  theme_bw() +
  scale_color_manual(values=c("firebrick1","forestgreen","darkblue"))








Here, we can see the result of grouping the real data by flower species. Now, setting up our algorithm:

set.seed(20)
irisCluster <- kmeans(iris[, 3:4], 3, nstart = 20)
irisCluster

library(grid)

actual = ggplot(iris, aes(Petal.Length, Petal.Width, color = Species)) + geom_point() +

Plotting within-cluster sum of squares

wssplot <- function(data, nc=15, seed=1234){
  wss <- (nrow(data)-1)*sum(apply(data,2,var))
  for (i in 2:nc){
    set.seed(seed)
    wss[i] <- sum(kmeans(data, centers=i)$withinss)}
  plot(1:nc, wss, type="b", xlab="Number of Clusters",
       ylab="Within groups sum of squares")
}

  cluster size ave.sil.width

Finally:

fviz_cluster(wine_cluster, data = wine_subset, ellipse.type = "norm") + 
  theme_bw() +
  theme(text = element_text(family="Georgia")) 








As a little bonus, we will do some hierarchical clustering with the crime data we started with.

The purpose of such clustering is to find the attribute of observations that have the smallest ‘eludican distance’ from one another, measured along some dimension of the data. Then build up from that with the next closest pairing, etc (although there are other possible building methods one could use). We first run the algorithm after scaling all variables and computing their ‘euclidean distance’ from each other along various dimensions, and then generate a dendrogram of the output:

library(cluster)
arrest.hc <- USArrests %>%
  scale() %>%                    
  dist(method = "euclidean") %>% 
  hclust(method = "ward.D2") 

fviz_dend(arrest.hc, k = 4, # Four groups
          cex = 0.5, 
          k_colors = c("firebrick1","forestgreen","blue", "purple"),
          color_labels_by_k = TRUE, # color labels by groups
          rect = TRUE, # Add rectangle (cluster) around groups,
          main = "Cluster Dendrogram: USA Arrest data"
) + theme(text = element_text(family="Georgia")) 








We can see certain states are found the be more ‘natural’ co-habitors via this process than others.

title: Stat Charts
text:
In order to make statistical charts in R, we can make use of a lot of the base ‘graphics’ package functions. For example, if I wanted to make a custom scatter-plot, I could code the following (after making some pseudo-random data to plot first):

x <- 1:45
y <- rnorm(n=45,mean=0,sd=1)

pseudo <- data.frame(x=x,y=y)
par(mai=c(1.4,1.4,1.4,1.4))
plot(pseudo$y~pseudo$x, col = "springgreen2", pch = c(1,2,3), cex = abs(pseudo$y),
     main = "Scatter Plot", 
     cex.main = 1,
     ylab ="Deviation",
     xlab="Trial",
     family = "serif")
mtext(text="Notice the lack of a clear pattern?", side =2, line = 6)








If I wanted to make a histogram of the same data’s ‘y’ values, I could go with:

hist(pseudo$y, main = "Example Histogram", xlab ="Deviation",
     col = "sienna2",
     breaks = 32,
     cex.main = 1.5,
     family = "serif")
text(x=2,y=8, expression(Freq.==frac(DRng,bar(YTot))))








Let say I wanted to make a vertical and horizontal bar-chart. This should be easiest using the ‘cars’ data-set that comes pre-packaged with R:

data(mtcars)
barplot(mtcars$hp, names = mtcars$gear,
        xlab = "Gear",
        ylab = "Horse-Power",
        main = "First Example Barchart",
        cex.main = 1.3,
        col = "olivedrab",
        family = "serif")







barplot(mtcars$hp, names = mtcars$gear,
        xlab = "Gear",
        ylab = "Horse-Power",
        main = "Second Example Barchart",
        cex.main = 1.3,
        col = "steelblue",
        horiz = TRUE,
        family = "serif")








How bout a boxplot? You ’betcha:

boxplot(disp ~ gear, data = mtcars,
        xlab = "Gear",
        ylab = "Disp",
        main = "Example Boxplot",
        varwidth = TRUE,
        notch = FALSE,
        boxwex = 0.4,
        col = "hotpink")








Finally, let’s finish with some pie-chart dessert (with a new example data set):

County <- c("Jackson", "Marrymay", "Bootswamp", "Alberta", "Janquin")
Population <- c(800, 900, 200, 150, 370)
top_ten <- data.frame(County, Population)
percent = Population/sum(Population) * 100
part_pie = paste (County, round(percent))
part_pie = paste0(part_pie, "%")
pie(top_ten$Population, labels = part_pie,
    col = c("purple", "violetred1", "green3",
            "cornsilk", "cyan", "white"),
    main = "Example Pie Chart")
legend("topleft", legend = c("Population"), bty = "y", pch = 20)








Now let us re-create these graphics in ggplot2! I’ll speed up the breakdown by displaying all of the graphs in a single panel.

library(ggplot2)
f <- ggplot(mpg, aes(x=displ, y=hwy, shape=drv))  +
  geom_point(size=4, aes(color= drv, size = manufacturer), alpha = 1/2) +
  geom_smooth(method = "loess") +
  labs(title = "Example Scatterplot") +
  labs(x = "Displ") +
  labs(y = "Hwy") +
  theme_light(base_family = "serif")

e <- ggplot(iris, aes(Species, Sepal.Length)) + 
  geom_boxplot() + 
  geom_dotplot(binaxis='y', 
               stackdir='center', 
               dotsize = .3, 
               fill= "royalblue") +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle=75, vjust=0.6)) + 
  labs(title="Example Box plot", 
       x="Species",
       y="Sepal Length") +
  theme_light(base_family = "serif")

d <- ggplot(iris, aes(Sepal.Length)) + scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Greens") +
     geom_histogram(aes(fill=Species), 
                   binwidth = .5, 
                   col="black", 
                   linewidth=.1) + 
  labs(title="Example Histogram",
       x="Sepal Length",
       y="Freq.") +
  theme_light(base_family = "serif")

c <- ggplot(iris, aes(Sepal.Width, fill=Species))+
  labs(title ="First Example Barchart",
       x = "Sepal.Width",
       y = "Freq.") + 
  scale_fill_brewer(palette="Set2") +
  theme_light(base_family = "serif") +
  geom_bar()

b <- ggplot(iris, aes(Sepal.Width, fill=Species, horiz = TRUE))+
  labs(title ="Second Example Barchart",
       x = "Sepal.Width",
       y = "Freq.") + 
  scale_fill_brewer(palette="Set2") +
  theme_light(base_family = "serif") +
  geom_bar() +
  coord_flip()

a <- ggplot(top_ten, aes(x="", y=Population, fill=County)) +
  geom_bar(stat="identity", width=1, color="white") +
  coord_polar("y", start=0)

library(cowplot)
plot_grid(a, b, c, d, e, f)








A nice panel. Be careful when exporting these graphics to different image formats such as .png or .jpeg. Often, one can save the output using a command such as:
ggsave(here("figures", "file_name.pdf"), plot = p_out,
height = 8, width = 10, units = "in")
You can change the output by appending ‘.jpeg’, ‘.svg’, ‘.tiff’, ‘.bmg’ among others to the file-name argument. It has been my experience that .png is probably best to strive for if your pipeline allows it, since it is often the best format to produce publication-quaility images of your plots.

title: Learning the See: Remarks on Dr. Tufte’s 2016 Keynote Session
Assignment_03
text:
Loading in the ‘TEDS_2016’ data-set, I attempt to run a regression on ‘Tondu’, selectively using ‘Age’, ‘Edu’, and ‘income’ as predictor variables. To do this, I use a command to create an function labeled ‘regplot’:

library(tidyverse)
library(haven)
TEDS_2016 <- read_stata("https://github.com/datageneration/home/blob/master/DataProgramming/data/TEDS_2016.dta?raw=true")
attach(TEDS_2016)
regplot=function(x,y,...){
  fit=lm(y~x)
  plot(x,y,...)
  abline(fit,col="red")
}
regplot(Tondu,age,xlab="age",ylab="Tondu",col="blue",pch=20)








Next, I cycle through each iteration with permutations of the following command in order to create and graph the regressions:

library(tidyverse)
library(haven)
TEDS_2016 <- read_stata("https://github.com/datageneration/home/blob/master/DataProgramming/data/TEDS_2016.dta?raw=true")
attach(TEDS_2016)
regplot(Tondu,income)







regplot(Tondu,Age)







regplot(Tondu,Edu)








But I would like to get a more comprehensive picture of the multivariate relationships I am dealing with. To try to better grapple with these, I construct a 3d plot, regressing ‘Tondu’ on both ‘income’ and ‘edu’ using:

TEDS_2016 <- read_stata("https://github.com/datageneration/home/blob/master/DataProgramming/data/TEDS_2016.dta?raw=true")
TEDS_2016$Tondu <- as.numeric(as.character(TEDS_2016$Tondu))
library(tidyverse)
library(plotly)
attach(TEDS_2016)
TEDS_2016_plot <- plot_ly(TEDS_2016,
                     x = income,
                     y = edu,
                     z = Tondu,
                     type = "scatter3d",
                     mode = "markers",
                     size = 0.02)
TEDS_2016_plot





An impressive visual, to say the least. But this relationship is not necessarily any clearer. What to do? Well, because I’ve used the variable dictionary within Rstudio, I can see that ‘Tondu’ really is a categorical variable. In other words, it is non-hierarchically ordered qualitative response predictor. Is there any sort of regression that can handle categorical dependent variables? There is: those that are known as ‘multinominal logit regressions’. Basically, a special type of log twist of standard OLS regression.

library(tidyverse)

title: Assignment_03
text:
Getting Log(istic)ical

As a first step, I convert ‘Tondu’ into a proper categorical variable with the ‘factor’ function:

TEDS_2016 <- read_stata("https://github.com/datageneration/home/blob/master/DataProgramming/data/TEDS_2016.dta?raw=true")
TEDS_2016=na.omit(TEDS_2016)
attach(TEDS_2016)
TEDS_2016$Tondu <- factor(TEDS_2016$Tondu, levels = c(1,2,3,4,5, 6, 9), labels = c("Unification now", "Status quo, unif. in future", "Status quo, decide later", "Status quo forever", "Status quo, indep. in future", "Independence now", "No response")) 

This also attaches the requite labels to the output levels of the variable. Now, MLRs have alot of fancy theory behind them which you can check up on here. I will be utilizing the modeling techniques described here. One good thing about MLRs is that we can treat explanatory ordinally structured variables as continuous.
So, proceeding ahead I make my MLR model with the ‘nnet’ library function ‘multinom’, and then run a summary:

library(nnet)
TEDDY <- multinom(Tondu ~ Age + Edu + income, data = TEDS_2016,
                  Hess = TRUE, trace = FALSE)
summary(TEDDY, Wald.ratios = TRUE)

Call:
multinom(formula = Tondu ~ Age + Edu + income, data = TEDS_2016, 
    Hess = TRUE, trace = FALSE)

Coefficients:
                             (Intercept)         Age         Edu       income
Status quo, unif. in future    -1.279791  0.32960034  0.57144475  0.073627701
Status quo, decide later        1.612667 -0.11181363  0.49000077  0.058884230
Status quo forever              1.031011  0.05532192  0.36193152  0.037564845
Status quo, indep. in future    1.640629 -0.27243191  0.53290606  0.063371340
Independence now                2.588714 -0.38408305  0.08254578 -0.008191805
No response                     0.525247  0.38905103 -0.64611163 -0.190700335

Std. Errors:
                             (Intercept)       Age       Edu     income
Status quo, unif. in future     1.516283 0.2671854 0.2444852 0.08959741
Status quo, decide later        1.462681 0.2584432 0.2382903 0.08657418
Status quo forever              1.483784 0.2622312 0.2409906 0.08771021
Status quo, indep. in future    1.473995 0.2600440 0.2410328 0.08761416
Independence now                1.569008 0.2774157 0.2595938 0.09466307
No response                     2.344280 0.4250818 0.3830006 0.12245914

Value/SE (Wald statistics):
                             (Intercept)        Age        Edu      income
Status quo, unif. in future   -0.8440320  1.2336018  2.3373390  0.82176148
Status quo, decide later       1.1025420 -0.4326430  2.0563190  0.68015925
Status quo forever             0.6948523  0.2109662  1.5018494  0.42828361
Status quo, indep. in future   1.1130494 -1.0476376  2.2109276  0.72330019
Independence now               1.6499050 -1.3845035  0.3179806 -0.08653644
No response                    0.2240547  0.9152380 -1.6869729 -1.55725685

Residual Deviance: 3319.504 
AIC: 3367.504 


Next, in order to make these results more amenable to probabilistic interpretation, I use the ‘predict’ function from the ‘car’ library:

predict(TEDDY, newdata = data.frame(Age = 1, Edu = 1, income = 1), type = 'probs')

             Unification now  Status quo, unif. in future 
                  0.03134793                   0.02310456 
    Status quo, decide later           Status quo forever 
                  0.24344397                   0.13851481 
Status quo, indep. in future             Independence now 
                  0.22354726                   0.30616791 
                 No response 
                  0.03387355 


Which means that when ‘Age’ = ‘Edu’ = ‘income’ = 1, the probability of supporting ‘Unification now’ goes us about 3.1%, supporting ‘Status quo forever’ goes up about 14%, etc.
A better way to get a wider probabilistic output is to use the ‘ggeffects’ library (note the other variables are specified according to their level-schemes: e.g. 1-5 with ‘base’ 1 in the case of ‘Age’, etc.):
#| warning: false
library(ggeffects)
ggeffect(TEDDY, terms = "Age[1:5,by=1]") 
ggeffect(TEDDY, terms = "Edu[1:5,by=1]")
ggeffect(TEDDY, terms = "income[1:10,by=1]")
Giving me the following output:

Which gives us the estimated probability of supporting, say, ‘Status quo, independence in the future’ for age bracket ‘1’ = 0.36 or 36%.
Finally, I will graph the probability outputs just derived (saving them as mark1, mark2, etc.) against the explanatory variables using variations of:
ggplot(mark1) +
  aes(x = x, y = predicted, fill = response.level, color = response.level) +
  geom_line() +
  geom_ribbon(aes(ymin = conf.low, ymax = conf.high), alpha = 1/3) +
  labs(x = 'Age', y = 'Predicted Probability') +
  ylim(c(0,1))
Which gives me the following outputs:






  



Showing the effect plots of levels of the explanatory variables associated with the probabilities of certain levels of ‘Tondu’.

title: EPPS 6356.501 - Final Project Dashboard
text:
#options(width = 1400)
library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)
library(maps)


Attaching package: 'maps'


The following object is masked from 'package:purrr':

    map

library(tibble)

trade_data <- read.csv("Merged_redux.csv")

region_mapping <- tibble(
  Region = c("North America", "North America", "North America", "South and Central America", "Europe & Eurasia", "Asia", "Asia",
             "South and Central America", "Asia", "Asia", "Africa", "Europe & Eurasia", "Africa", "Middle East",  "Europe & Eurasia", "Middle East"),
  Country = c("United States", "China", "Russia", "United States", "China", "Russia",
              "United States", "China", "Russia", "United States", "China", "Russia",
              "United States", "China", "Russia", "United States"),
  Partner = c("Afghanistan", "Belarus", "Bangladesh", "India", "Iran", "Bangladesh", "India", "Iran",
               "Iraq", "Israel", "Japan", "Myanmar", "Pakistan", "Qatar", "Saudi Arabia", "South Korea"),
  Latitude = c(37.0902, 56.130366, 23.634501, -14.235004, 51.165691, 35.8617, 20.5937,
               -1.8312, 23.6850, 30.3753,9.0820, 52.1326, -1.2864, 15.552727, 55.378051,23.885942),
  Longitude = c(-95.7129,  -106.346771, -102.552784, -51.92528, 10.451526, 104.1954, 78.96288,
                -78.1834, 90.3563, 69.3451, 8.6753, 5.2913,37.9062, 48.5164,-3.4359, 45.0792))

trade_data_coords <- trade_data %>%
  left_join(region_mapping, by = c("Supplier" = "Country")) %>%
  rename(Export_Lat = Latitude, Export_Lon = Longitude, Region_Export = Supplier) %>%
  left_join(region_mapping, by = c("Recipient" = "Partner")) %>%
  rename(Import_Lat = Latitude, Import_Lon = Longitude, Region_Import = Recipient)

Warning in left_join(., region_mapping, by = c(Supplier = "Country")): Detected an unexpected many-to-many relationship between `x` and `y`.
ℹ Row 1 of `x` matches multiple rows in `y`.
ℹ Row 2 of `y` matches multiple rows in `x`.
ℹ If a many-to-many relationship is expected, set `relationship =
  "many-to-many"` to silence this warning.


Warning in left_join(., region_mapping, by = c(Recipient = "Partner")): Detected an unexpected many-to-many relationship between `x` and `y`.
ℹ Row 1 of `x` matches multiple rows in `y`.
ℹ Row 3 of `y` matches multiple rows in `x`.
ℹ If a many-to-many relationship is expected, set `relationship =
  "many-to-many"` to silence this warning.

trade_data_clean = trade_data_coords %>%
  filter(!is.na(Region_Export), !is.na(Region_Import)) %>%
  mutate(Route = paste(Region_Export, "to", Region_Import)) %>%
  filter(!is.na(Export_Lon), !is.na(Export_Lat), !is.na(Import_Lon), !is.na(Import_Lat))  %>%
  filter(!(Export_Lon == Import_Lon & Export_Lat == Import_Lat))

# create map
world <- map_data("world")

world_regions <- world %>%
  mutate(Region = case_when(
    region %in% c("USA", "Canada", "Mexico") ~ "North America",
    region %in% c("Brazil", "Argentina", "Ecuador") ~ "South and Central America",
    region %in% c("Germany", "Russia", "France", "Netherlands", "UK") ~ "Europe & Eurasia",
    region %in% c("China", "India", "Bangladesh", "Pakistan") ~ "Asia",
    region %in% c("Japan", "Australia") ~ "Asia Pacific",
    region %in% c("Nigeria", "South Africa", "Kenya") ~ "Africa",
    region %in% c("Saudi Arabia", "Yemen") ~ "Middle East",
    TRUE ~ "Other"
  ))
# Color palette for regions
region_colors <- c(
  "North America" = "mistyrose1",
  "South and Central America" = "tomato",
  "Europe & Eurasia" = "lightskyblue3",
  "Asia" = "antiquewhite",
  "Asia Pacific" = "sandybrown",
  "Middle East" = "pink",
  "Africa" = "aquamarine",
  "Other" = "gray90")

# Create base map
ggplot() +
  geom_polygon(data = world_regions, aes(x = long, y = lat, group = group, fill = Region), color = "black") +
  geom_curve(data = trade_data_clean, aes(x = Export_Lon, y = Export_Lat,
                                          xend = Import_Lon, yend = Import_Lat, color = Route, size = Amount), 
             size = 0.3 ,curvature = 0.1, arrow = arrow(length = unit(0.1, "cm"))) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = region_colors, name = "Region") +  
  coord_fixed(1.3) +
  labs(title = "Routes of Arms Partners",
       x = "Longitude",
       y = "Latitude",
       subtitle = "A Global 'Network' View",
       caption = "Sources: WITS and SIPRI") +
  theme_few() +
  theme(text = element_text(family = "Optima")) +
  theme(legend.position = "right") +
  theme(legend.key.size = unit(0.5, 'cm'))

Warning: Using `size` aesthetic for lines was deprecated in ggplot2 3.4.0.
ℹ Please use `linewidth` instead.













library(hrbrthemes)
fult22 <- fult2 %>% filter(Armament != 'NA' )
fult222 <- fult22 %>% filter(Armament != 'Other')

f2 <- fult222 %>%
  arrange(desc(SIPRI.estimate)) %>%
  #mutate(country = factor(country, country)) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x=Amount, y=TII.Score, size=TIV.delivery.values, color=Armament)) +
  geom_point(alpha=0.5) +
  scale_colour_ft() +
  scale_x_log10(labels=scales::dollar) +
  scale_size(range = c(.1, 24), name="") +
  dark_theme_light() +
  theme(text = element_text(family = "Optima")) +
  labs(x = "Overall Trade Amount per Year (Logged, Thousand $)", y = "TII Rank",
       title = "Trade Flows and Weapons by 'Supplier'",
       subtitle = "How Trade Levels Relate to\nWeapons Transfered (sans 'Other')\nImplicit TIV Value Range",
       caption = "Sources: SIPRI and WITS") +
  geom_hline(aes(yintercept=1.67),
             color="red", linetype="dashed", linewidth=0.5) +
  facet_wrap(~Country, ncol = 2)

Inverted geom defaults of fill and color/colour.
To change them back, use invert_geom_defaults().

ggplotly(f2)

title: McGhee and HPI
text:
Geoff McGee is a journalist who has spent his career building data visuals. He has also produced an informative documentary on the opportunities and challenges data visualizations bring in the documentary Journalism in the Age of Data (2011).
The central insight of this piece for those who would practice data visualization is: use the visuals to tell a meaningful narrative. Beauty is not for beauty’s sake here, but for utility’s. Software will allow anyone to make meaningless connections look astounding. Given the way the human mind interprets visual information, if something is ‘seen’ it is usually because an understanding is being conveyed. However faulty.
There is a lot of rich advice in the documentary, so be sure to check it out yourself. Though made with a focus on journalism, that does not preclude the appropriateness of it’s precepts. Among the many experts interviewed throughout the piece, Jeffery Steer (then professor at Standford) cautions that exploratory data visualizations should be able to be produced quickly to allow rapid publiciation. But this advice can be adopted by others as a threshold heuristic: if it takes you weeks to peek behind the data, perhaps there is a better way of looking through it? Renowned data visualizer and statistician Amanda Cox concurs, noting that an ability to “sketch with data” is a prequiste step towards making any sense out of it.
Now, we can also plot some graphics of our own using the Happy Planet Index (HPI). This data-set tracks measures of well-being across the globe. You can learn more here. After downloading the index from 2023 in .csv format and cleaning it up a tad, we can start plotting. Let us actually start with a 3-D plot:

hpi <- read.csv("hpi_onesheet.csv")
xyz <- unique(cbind(hpi$HPI,hpi$GDP,hpi$CO2))

persp(xyz,
      phi = 20, theta = -44,
      xlab = "HPI Rank",
      ylab = "GDP ($)",
      zlab = "Co2 Levels",
      main = "A 3D Distribution",
      col = "skyblue", border = NULL, ltheta = -135, lphi = 0,
      shade = 0.75, box = TRUE, axes = TRUE, nticks = 5,
      d = 2)








Next, a lattice style plot:

pairs(~HPI + Population + GDP, data = hpi,
      main = "A Lattice Style Plot")








While we are at it, a lines graph:

par(new = FALSE, mai = c(.8,.8,.8,.8))
plot(hpi$Population~hpi$GDP, col = "red", type = "l",
     xlab = "GDP ($)",
     ylab = "Population",
     main = "Lines Plot")

par(new = TRUE)
plot(hpi$Population~hpi$Carbon, col = "blue", type = "l",
      xaxt = "n",
      ylab = "",
      xlab = "")
axis(side = 4)
mtext(text = "Carbon Levels", side =4, line = 3)
legend("topright", legend = c("Pop vs. GDP", "Pop vs. Co2"),
       col = c("red", "blue"),
       lty = c(1, 1), bty = "n")








Let us go ahead and make scatter-plot too:

plot(hpi$Population, hpi$GDP, col = "hotpink", pch = 16,
     log='x',
     cex = 0.7,
     xlab = "Log GDP ($)",
     ylab = "Population",
     main = "Population vs. GDP",
     bty="n")








How meaningful are these graphs? Well, though simplistic, they do seem to reveal some correlations between between facets of our HPI data-set. Save for the last one, at least. If we were undertaking a full analysis here, we would next try to parse and possibly condition our data in more refined models to see if this relationship holds under differing parameterizations (which are ultimately a new set of assumptions about how our variables relate to each other). Or with different data.
The range of graphic elements can allow us to peer into many masks of our data. The differences that can be shown (or conjured) by lines, areas or contours can pipe directly into how communicable our narratives are. Our goal now is to unmask whatever face truth is wearing with these tools, assuming it is at the masquerade.

title: Assignment Four
text:
Ever wanted to know how to make a variable width column chart, a table with embedded charts, a bar chart, or a column chart in R? Well, look no further than below! Just follow these codes and you too can graphically represent your data in a stunning variety of ways.
We will start off loading in some libraries:

# Load in libraries
# Be sure they are installed first
library(tidyverse)
library(hrbrthemes)
library(gt)
library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)
library(tidyr)
library(hrbrthemes)
library(scales)

If you have some categories with different amounts of observations for each of them, and need to plot two variables per category, you can make a variable width column chart:

# Read in occupational data-set:
data2 <- read.csv("occs.csv")
data3 <- data2[-c(6:16), ] # Pare down the data to make it  managable

# Change variables:
attach(data3)
Occupation <- as.factor(Occupation)
SOC <- as.factor(SOC)

# Cumsum the change bar widths
data3$right <- cumsum(data3$Hires) + 30*c(0:(nrow(data3)-1))
data3$left <- data3$right - data3$Hires

# Make actual var-width barplot:
ggplot(data3, aes(ymin = 0)) + 
   geom_rect(aes(xmin = left, xmax = right, ymax = Earnings, colour = Occupation, fill = Occupation)) +
   xlab("Count (#) of Hires") + 
   ylab("$/per Hour") +
   ggtitle("Number of Hires vs. Wages in DFW (June 2024)") +
   theme_ipsum() +
   theme(legend.position="bottom",
         legend.text=element_text(size=7),
         axis.title.x = element_text(size = 12, hjust = 0.5),
         axis.title.y = element_text(size = 12, hjust = 0.5, vjust = 2.5),
         plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5),
         legend.background = element_blank(),
        legend.key.size = unit(0.000000000004, 'cm'),
         legend.key.height = unit(0.00006, 'cm'),
         legend.key.width = unit(0.00006, 'cm'),
         legend.box.background = element_rect(colour = "black"))








Wow, Nice! Ever wanted to plot a bunch of graphics in a single table? Here is how you can:

# Load a new dataset
data <- read.csv("oneside.csv")

# List countries
selected_countries <- c("India", "Pakistan", "Afghanistan", "Nigeria", 
                        "Yemen", "Iraq", "Burundi", "Sierra Leonne", "Colombia", 
                        "Philippines", "Mexico")

# Table w/ embedded charts:

# Filter data 
filtered_data <- data %>%
  filter(location %in% selected_countries)

# Summ. fatality 
summary_data <- filtered_data %>%
  group_by(location, year) %>%
  summarise(
    avg_fatality = mean(best_fatality_estimate, na.rm = TRUE)
  ) %>%
  pivot_longer(cols = avg_fatality,
               names_to = "fatality_type",
               values_to = "fatality")

# Combine plots
ggplot(summary_data, aes(x = year, y = fatality, color = location, group = location)) +
  geom_line(size = 1.2) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  labs(title = "Average Fatality Estimates by Country Over the Years",
       subtitle = "Selected Countries",
       x = "Year",
       y = "Average Fatality Estimates",
       color = "Country") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 15) +
  scale_color_brewer(palette = "Set1") +  # Use a distinct color palette
  theme(
    legend.position = "bottom",
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 20),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 15),
    axis.title = element_text(face = "bold"),
    axis.text = element_text(size = 12, angle = 45, hjust = 1)
  ) +
  facet_wrap(~ location, scales = "free_y")  # Separate plots for each country with free y-scales








Now if you have a lot of items to compare, you can make a column chart:

# reload international dataset as new df
mydata <- read.csv("oneside.csv")


# List new countries
selected_countries <- c("India", "Pakistan", "Afghanistan", "Nigeria", 
                        "Yemen", "Iraq", "Burundi", "Sierra Leone", 
                        "Colombia", "Philippines", "Mexico")

# Filter 
filtered_data <- mydata %>%
  filter(location %in% selected_countries)

# Summarize
summary_data <- filtered_data %>%
  group_by(location) %>%
  summarise(
    avg_fatality = mean(best_fatality_estimate, na.rm = TRUE),
    low_fatality = mean(low_fatality_estimate, na.rm = TRUE)
  ) %>%
  pivot_longer(cols = c(avg_fatality, low_fatality),
               names_to = "fatality_type",
               values_to = "fatality")

# Make chart 
ggplot(summary_data, aes(x = location)) +
  geom_bar(aes(y = fatality, fill = fatality_type), 
           position = position_dodge(width = 0.6),  # Adjust width for overlap
           stat = "identity", width = 0.6, color = "black") +  # Set bar width
  labs(title = "Fatality Estimates by Country",
       x = "Country",
       y = "Fatality Estimates",
       fill = "Fatality Type") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 15) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("avg_fatality" = "steelblue", 
                               "low_fatality" = "forestgreen")) +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1))  # Slant x-axis labels








And here is a way to make good old fashioned bar charts:

hh <- ggplot(data2) +
      ggtitle("June 2024 Hires in DFW") +
      geom_col(aes(Occupation, Hires), fill='blue', width=0.7) +
      scale_x_discrete(labels = function(x) str_wrap(x, width =        55)) +
      coord_flip()
hh








There is practically no limit to what one can graph in R.

title: Assignment Two
text:
Some Graphics:
Graphing visual information can reveal extensive and unexpected relationships in data-sets. Below, we will post the output of some reproductions of examples from Murrell’s (2019) reference book. First, let us plot the relationship between ‘pressure’ and ‘temperature’ in R (the point shapes of which we can change by tweaking the ‘pch’ parameter):





Below, we have made a scatter-plot of of bird travel times compared to responses per travel and per second. We are able to change the size of points by varying the ‘cex’ parameter. I have also rendered the inner-background of the ‘lower’ line of dots blue. We can also change the scale and display of each axis by messing around with functions like ‘axis(1, at=seq(0, 16, 4))’(Note: ‘1’ in this command represents the x-axis which we are here changing). We can also make a histogram, this time cmapring some randomly generated but normally spread data:





















Finally, we conjure up (from top-left going right) a box-plot, bar-pot, a 3D surface chart and a pie-chart:





































All of the above graphics can be produced using a native R distro. Now, we can try using some of these ‘in-built’ tools to help us see some relationships in another data-set. Specifically, we will be looking at the Happy Planet Index (HPI) data-set for 2024. You can learn more about this data-set by clicking here. After cleaning up the original excel sheets a tad by turning them into .csv files and removing some observations with missing data, we can produce the following output:





































Some intriguing examples to be sure. We can see the rather apparent lack of a relationship between GDP and HPI (and the residuals of plotting them against each other), that lower carbon levels correlate with longer life-spans, and the quartile distribution of GDP per capita when stacked against population levels. Not bad for a few lines of code.
Let us also append a quick overview of some of the most import graphing functions that comes along with base R’s graphics package:
par() = A function with which to tinker with wider functionaility of plot parameters. Such as figure height, axis scale, etc.
lines() = A function to join points to lines.
points() = A simple function to points at various coordinates.
axis() = A function to add an axis to a graph.
box() = A function to draw a box around a plot.
text() = A function to add text to a graph/visual.
mtext() = A function to write text in the margins of the graph.
hist() = A function to draw a histogram of a variable in a data-set.
boxplot() = A function to make box-plots from a data-set.
legend() = Legend specifications for a graphic can be set with this function.
persp() = A function to draw 3-D plots.
names() = A quick function to find or set the names of objects in a. data-set.
pie() = A function that allows to graph pie-charts.

title: Two Styles
text:
Take a look at these two graphics:





















The graphic on the left immediately comes across as a tad more stylistic than that on the right. However, each seeks to participate in the enterprise of producing insight. The leftmost maps the correlation of invasive seaweed and the number of smaller sea creatures. The right plots different ages major authors published notable books. These visuals are the works of Albert Cario (left), a journalist, professor and graphic trend-setter (some pun intended) and Colin Ware, a psychologist who has spent his career working at the intersection of computer science and ocean studies. The summary on this page will be upon their respective books: Cario’s The Functional Art and Ware’s Visual Thinking for Information Design.
Each explicitly adheres to a philosophy of seeing as the best route the human understanding. Both attempt to leverage the perceptual apparatus of human cortexes in order to assist with them in the task of imbibing concepts in the minds of those who perceive with them. For them, the ocular root of the word insight is no accident. Human cognition is greatly built upon, and functions through, spatio-visual models of the world. The more information can be synthesized via these pathways, the more easily (and efficiently) such information can be understood. The total architecture of our perceptual apparatus and the modalities of filtration (attention) that this instantiates in each of us can therefore form a foundation for precepts of design and communication.



Cario’s “Island of Knowledge’


Cario’s life project has been one of using functionality to assist us in our voyages to the never reachable horizon of truth. For him, the functional is often a proxy (or at least a clue) for veracity because good explanations usually share a coherent structure – at least to the understanding human. So, visual analyses that attempt to account for the great depth and breadth of a subject, and can do so compellingly, have a greater chance of withstanding scrutiny because it is harder for such an enterprise to be logically congruent with more spurious associations.



An extract from Ware


Ware’s ambitions parrel those of Cario’s to an extent. Unlike Cario’s relatively philosophic focus on the journalist enterprise of truth exposition, he focuses more on how the biological mechanics of perception literally structure and pipeline to higher human cognition. For example, he writes about how opponent process theory helps explain the differential reactions to certain combinations of colors - which should inform graphical presentations. So we can, say, appreciate how chromatic contrast limits how many useful juxtapositions can be easily relayed with color within a graphic.
Both authors have produced remarkable (and yes, beautiful) books that should be consulted by anyone who wants to effectively communicate what their analysis of data reveals. Which in this century, should be just about everyone.

title: Knuth and Malinowski
text:
Back in the 1980s, computer scientist Don Knuth established an approach to writing programs that emphasized their human interpretability. Structuring code in this way is supposed to allow humans to focus on the connective implementation issues of choosing how to make the best program to do whatever a human needs any given program to do.
Knuth’s stylistic philosophy has been most famously emphasized in a 1984 article he wrote for The Computer Journal. There, he notes that his goal was to build on the functional programming approach that came to fore in the 1970s and provide a methodology of composition that would inform the next generation of programming as a practice. Given the citation impact that 1984 article (and Knuth’s wider body of work) has had, it is clear he has unequivocally succeeded in getting his message across.
He recommends coding programs in a language of his own invention that can be used to be to produce two outputs for any programming process. A human readable document and a machine-readable one. His denotes this approach doing things via WEB programming. When written in the WEB language, ideally, these two outputs can be automatically produced as end-products from a WEB file.
By first being implemented via the WEB language, it is hoped that the program written is logical, well-connected, and easily understandable to any other programmer who comes along and looks at it. This is mainly because writing in WEB is done in an expository style that forces one to write code relatively clearly.
In his 1984 paper Knuth makes clear that he sees his particular way of making our programs, the WEB way, as well-suited for the building approaches that actual programmers use. Rather than emphasizing top-down or bottom-up approaches, programs are more naturally instantiated via WEB as, well, webs. Or: interconnected structures and their relationships. It is left up to each individual programmer to choose whether to build those connections from a bird’s or a mole’s eye (ignore any second-thoughts) view.

Stephen Malinowski is a software engineer and composer whose has made some impressive music visualizations from MIDI files. Watchable on YouTube, his dynamic graphics use length, height and color to help break down the composition of compositions.
His work is of a piece with other analyses via musicological visualizations. As noted by Issacson (2005), a range of tools have been developed to help bring the structure of music to light in a way formal notation sheets might not. Although he observes that many such graphics are construed based off of artistic assumptions of what is relevant about a given piece of, usually orchestral, music.



From Issacson What You See Is What You Get: On Visualizing Music (2005)


Of course, the fundamental role of the perceptual building blocks of most graphics such as shapes and colors have preoccupied theorists and researchers of many stripes as long as data has been displayed. Currently, the prescriptions of the likes of Edward Tufte and Leland Wilkinson inform the practice of most contemporary data analysts. As noted by Robert Cario, when it comes to presenting their analyses, data practitioners (should) align with journalists in a common endeavor to provide a window into ‘Truth’. Elements of visual composition can assist in making data’s melodies ring as clefs make those of dead composers do.
For example, it is currently the consensus that human perception can better filter and build intuitive perceptions off certain pairings of colors than with others. Specifically, it appears that black, white, red, green, yellow, and blue are rather primal constituent colors of human perception. The pairing, and especially contrasting, of these basic colors form the modality of much of what experiencing the world with color (including via grey-scale) consists of.



From Ware (2022)


For more, check out Colin Ware’s excellent Visual Thinking For Information Design (2022).

title: Wickham and ‘Google Flu’
text:
Stuffy Algorithms
Data analysis requires us to be aware of the possibly of spurious correlations made as much out of ‘noise’ as any real ‘data’. Such an issue, known as overfitting, infamously tanked Google’s ‘Google Flu’ project, the search giant’s algorithmic enterprise to predict flu patterns.
As documented in David Lazer, Ryan Kennedy, Gary King, and Alessandro Vespignani ‘s article “The Parable of Google Flu: Traps in Big Data Analysis”, an over-presumption in the efficacy of large sets of data to always mend itself to truthful inquiry likely played a role in the predictive shortcomings ‘Google Flu’ became known for. Especially for ever changing data sets such as search engine queries and results, the likelihood of ‘overparameterization’ (too many features on a comparably small number of observations) ‘revealing’ spurious correlations to real trends of interest is likely to be a serious pitiful to truthful generalization.
The lesson Lazer et al. provide suggests that the limitations of inference due to unworthy data is not just a problem of traditional statistical modeling on limited data sets, but likely a universal constraint on analyses of any size of information.


On Wickham’s Keynote
Back in 2019, Dr. Hadley Wickham gave a keynote presentation at the EMBL Advanced Training Centre in Germany. During that talk, he emphasized a few key points in regard to visual presentations of data:

Visualizations are (really) functions
More work is required to undertake expository analysis, as opposed to exploratory analysis
‘Stack’ your visuals via orthogonal components
There are advantages to contextualizing visualizations as a part of the coding process because:

Code is text
Code is read-able
Code is reproducible




Watch the talk here: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=9YTNYT1maa4.
Each of these precepts embody some aspect of the relational integration of visualizations with the goal of data analysis, which is to uncover true facts about the world. Taking point the point about orthogonality, for instance, emphasizes the different facets of a data-set that can be displayed with (or perhaps against) each other for the purpose of better illuminating how they both speak to a given fact of reality. For example, GDP levels for each nation over time, filtered by size of underlying population, may bespeak of the higher returns on lifespan that industrialization tends to bring communities that can wisely harness it.
Perhaps most novelly, Dr. Wickham really points to the fact that visualizations are the product of the same types of manipulations that summary statistics tend to be: a.k.a algorithms instantiated via pieces code. This empathizes that rather being a class apart, visual relationships are to be got to in much the same manner as other deliverables in data analysis.

title: Web Scraping
text:
Web-scraping is still one of those tools that almost seems to sweat drops of black magic. For instance, with just a few lines of code I can make a data-frame of foreign-exchange reserves by country from Wikipedia. First, visit the following Wikipedia portal: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_countries_by_foreign-exchange_reserves. This should lead you to the page reprinted below:

Now, you can copy the code below to create a ‘fores’ object from XML data siphoned from this page (you’ll need to use developer tools and find the ‘table_class’ associated with the country listings table). Then, you can turn this data into a .csv of quite a few countries foreign reserves for 2024!

The code:
library(tidyverse)
library(rvest)

url <- 'https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_countries_by_foreign-exchange_reserves'

wikiforreserve <- read_html(url)
class(wikiforreserve)

foreignreserve <- wikiforreserve %>%
  html_nodes(xpath='//*[@id="mw-content-text"]/div[1]/table[1]') %>%
  html_table()
class(foreignreserve) 

fores = foreignreserve[[1]][,c(1, 2,3,4,5,6,7,8) ]

names(fores) <- c("Country", "Forexreswithgold", "Date1", "Change1","Forexreswithoutgold", "Date2","Change2", "Sources")
colnames(fores)

head(fores$Country, n=10)

fores$Date1 = as.Date(fores$Date1, format = "%d %b %Y")
class(fores$Date1)

write.csv(fores, "fores.csv", row.names = FALSE) # use fwrite?
I also made a .csv of a list of empires from this page: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_empires.

We can also ‘nab some official documents while we are at it. The documents in question come from https://www.govinfo.gov/app/search/. However, given the complexity of the code, I will spare you the run-through here. Basically, you can use the ’purr’, ‘magrittr’ and ‘jsonlite’ packages in R to grab the data. Suffice to say to download 5 documents was a half-a gig job that took a couple of minutes, so your millage might vary. But to download seven rather light docs from the 118th congressional session only took a few seconds. Regardless, here is a snippet of what I was able to reel up:

Now, one criticism that might be leveled against the scraping method used above is that it cannot recognize all character/typefaces. For instance, the empire .csv seems to contain a few importing errors in the start and end year columns. One way to improve these results might be to use the ‘janitor’ package to clean up things like col. names, although this may not hit most of the issues encountered when scraping from any given web page. As always, know your data!

title: Assignment 3
text:
We can use the ‘Quanteda’ package for R to analyze tweets made during the summit that took place with Joe Biden and Xi Jinping back in 2021. First we import our data set of tweets, token-ize it, and cast the tokened break-down into a document-feature matrix (DTM).

library(quanteda)

library(quanteda.textplots)
library(readr)
library(ggplot2)

summit <- read_csv("https://raw.githubusercontent.com/datageneration/datamethods/master/textanalytics/summit_11162021.csv")
View(summit)

sum_twt = summit$text
toks = tokens(sum_twt)
sumtwtdfm <- dfm(toks)
class(toks)

Now, we can also analyze past presidential inauguration speeches to search for trends in topics discussed by incoming candidates or incumbents after the end of each election cycle.
Let us load in the speeches data-set, and look at the most common words used by Bush, Obama and Trump for their speeches:

dfm_inaug <- corpus_subset(data_corpus_inaugural, Year <= 1826) %>% 
  tokens(remove_punct = TRUE) %>% 
  tokens_remove(stopwords('english')) %>% 
  dfm() %>%
  dfm_trim(min_termfreq = 10, verbose = FALSE)

set.seed(100)
textplot_wordcloud(dfm_inaug)







inaug_speech = data_corpus_inaugural

corpus_subset(data_corpus_inaugural, 
              President %in% c("Trump", "Obama", "Bush")) %>%
  tokens(remove_punct = TRUE) %>%
  tokens_remove(stopwords("english")) %>%
  dfm() %>%
  dfm_group(groups = President) %>%
  dfm_trim(min_termfreq = 5, verbose = FALSE) %>%
  textplot_wordcloud(comparison = TRUE)








We can also examine the frequency with which presidents inaugurated since 1949 have used the terms ‘american’, ‘people’, and ‘jobs’.

data_corpus_inaugural_subset <- 
  corpus_subset(data_corpus_inaugural, Year > 1949)
kwic(tokens(data_corpus_inaugural_subset), pattern = "american") %>%
  textplot_xray()







textplot_xray(
  kwic(tokens(data_corpus_inaugural_subset), pattern = "american"),
  kwic(tokens(data_corpus_inaugural_subset), pattern = "people"),
  kwic(tokens(data_corpus_inaugural_subset), pattern = "jobs")
  
)

  kwic(tokens(data_corpus_inaugural_subset), pattern = "american"),

)

  theme(legend.position = "none")








We also make more focused plots of how often the four most recent presidents have used the term ‘american’. After we pair down wider feature views of relative term frequencies, below:

library(quanteda.textstats)
features_dfm_inaug <- textstat_frequency(dfm_inaug, n = 100)

# Sort by reverse frequency order
features_dfm_inaug$feature <- with(features_dfm_inaug, reorder(feature, -frequency))

ggplot(features_dfm_inaug, aes(x = feature, y = frequency)) +
  geom_point() + 
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 90, hjust = 1))







# Get frequency grouped by president
freq_grouped <- textstat_frequency(dfm(tokens(data_corpus_inaugural_subset)), 
                                   groups = data_corpus_inaugural_subset$President)

# Filter the term "american"
freq_american <- subset(freq_grouped, freq_grouped$feature %in% "american")  

ggplot(freq_american, aes(x = group, y = frequency)) +
  geom_point() + 
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0, 14), breaks = c(seq(0, 14, 2))) +
  xlab(NULL) + 
  ylab("Frequency") +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 90, hjust = 1))

  geom_point() +

Let us also compare word usage by Obama and Trump:

pres_corpus <- corpus_subset(data_corpus_inaugural, 
                             President %in% c("Obama", "Trump"))

# Create a dfm grouped by president
pres_dfm <- tokens(pres_corpus, remove_punct = TRUE) %>%
  tokens_remove(stopwords("english")) %>%
  tokens_group(groups = President) %>%
  dfm()

# Calculate keyness and determine Trump as target group
result_keyness <- textstat_keyness(pres_dfm, target = "Trump")

# Plot estimated word keyness
textplot_keyness(result_keyness)








We can see, for instance, that Trump used more forceful and relatively less idealistic wordage than Obama for his inaugural speech.
If desired, we could also model probabilities derived from text data using unsupervised machine learning methods such as Wordfish. Wordfish can look at a set of documents, and based off of the incidence of certain words, estimate where in the set a given document likely is. This can be useful for an analysis that might make time series presumptions concerning a given document-set, for example.
Literature is often an inherently unstructured source of information. At least compared to the usual numeric tabular data entries that a lot of data analysis tools are built for. But with statistical algorithmic approaches, the quantification of this most naturally human output can be brought under the purview of information extraction techniques.
